video_title: US-Iran Deal Triggers Rally in Asian Stocks | The China Show 6/15/2026
gist: Bloomberg's The China Show covers Asian markets rallying on a tentative US-Iran interim deal to reopen the Strait of Hormuz (to be signed Fri June 19, then 60 days of talks), with Brent near $80-84 and broad EM/tech relief; guests stress the deal is fragile and oil inventories near depletion by early August, while a separate thread covers a Trump-administration order forcing Anthropic to suspend its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 AI models. (Note: video title labels this 'Odd Lots · Bloomberg' but the transcript is Bloomberg's 'The China Show' with hosts David and Yvonne.)

insights.claim: The US and Iran have reached an interim agreement to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, with a memorandum of understanding to be signed in Switzerland on Friday June 19, which then starts a 60-day clock to negotiate a final deal covering the harder issues like Iran's nuclear program.
insights.speaker: John Herskovitz, Bloomberg East Asia governments editor
insights.so_what: Friday signing is the binary catalyst markets are pricing; the 60-day window is where the real risk to the peace trade lives.
insights.quote: WHEN THE MEMORANDUM OF UNDERSTANDING IS SET, IT STARTS A 60 DAY CLOCK TO REACH A FINAL AGREEMENT.

insights.claim: Iran's signing reportedly presumes $2 billion of unfrozen funds transferred up front before any Hormuz reopening and another $2 billion 60 days later, plus an end to Israeli strikes on Lebanon/Hezbollah, conditions that may not be met before Friday.
insights.speaker: Robert Pape, professor of political science, University of Chicago
insights.so_what: Watch the upfront $2B transfer and a 5-day pause in Israeli strikes as concrete pre-conditions; failure on either could unwind the relief rally before Friday.
insights.quote: THERE WILL BE SOME 2 BILLION TRANSFERRED OF THE UNFROZEN FUNDS OF IRAN TRANSFERRED RIGHT UP FRONT BEFORE ANY OPENING OF HORMUZ OCCURS. AND 60 DAYS LATER, ANOTHER 2 BILLION.

insights.claim: Bloomberg Economics assesses the odds of any US-Iran deal lasting as low, arguing both sides are likely to use a lull in fighting to prepare for the next round of hostilities.
insights.speaker: David (host, citing Bloomberg Economics)
insights.so_what: House view is that the rally is a relief bounce, not a durable regime change; fade or hedge positions that assume permanent de-escalation.
insights.quote: WE ASSESS THE ODDS FOR ANY DEAL LAST DATE ARE LOW. BOTH SIDES ARE LIKELY TO USE ANY LULL IN THE FIGHTING TO PREPARE FOR THE NEXT ROUND OF HOSTILITIES.

insights.claim: The conflict is in a 'middle game' not an endgame, because Iran will use its demonstrated leverage over the Strait of Hormuz to push for US naval withdrawal and base closures, not merely to resume selling oil.
insights.speaker: Robert Pape, professor of political science, University of Chicago
insights.so_what: Frames the deal as a leverage negotiation that escalates Iran's demands over time, implying repeated headline risk and a structurally higher Hormuz risk premium.
insights.quote: WE ARE IN THE MIDDLE GAME. I KNOW EVERYBODY WANTS IT TO BE THE END GAME.

insights.claim: Iran's Hormuz leverage is permanent and will grow over the next 30-60 days, because it has shown it can close the strait with a handful of drones or even emails to insurance companies, and oil cannot physically reach refineries for 30-60 days even after reopening.
insights.speaker: Robert Pape, professor of political science, University of Chicago
insights.so_what: Even a signed deal does not remove the recurring closure option, so oil-tail-risk hedges and a residual war premium remain justified.
insights.quote: IRAN HAS DISCOVERED IT CAN CLOSE THE STRAIT OF HORMUZ WHENEVER IT WANTS FOR ANY REASON IT WANTS WITH JUST A HANDFUL OF DRONES. IT MIGHT BE ABLE TO DO IT WITH A HANDFUL OF EMAILS TO THE INSURANCE COMPANIES.

insights.claim: The key real-world indicator of genuine de-escalation is whether Trump withdraws 50-70% of naval assets and the airborne/Marine troops moved to the region back to bases in Asia and California; he has not committed to doing so.
insights.speaker: Robert Pape, professor of political science, University of Chicago
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete, observable signal to validate the peace trade beyond headlines; absent troop drawdown, treat the rally as unconfirmed.
insights.quote: IF IT TURNS OUT THAT PRESIDENT TRUMP DOESN'T JUST END THE BLOCKADE BUT WITHDRAWS 50 TO 70% OF THE NAVAL ASSETS THAT HAVE BEEN MOVED TO THE REGION, THAT IS A HUGE INDICATOR.

insights.claim: Conflicting public statements (Iran says the US 'balked' at its requests; the US calls it a strategic win) and the refusal of both sides to release their agreed texts signal mistrust and unresolved terms, making a clean Friday signing uncertain.
insights.speaker: Abeer (Bloomberg, Dubai)
insights.so_what: The absence of a released draft is itself the tell; investors should treat the 'deal' as a non-binding intention until text is public.
insights.quote: I THINK WE HAVE NOT QUITE A MEMORANDUM OF UNDERSTANDING. I THINK WE HAVE A MEMORANDUM OF CONFUSION.

insights.claim: Trump said the US blockade would be lifted immediately and Iran could get a 60-day license to sell oil without restrictions, while future Strait of Hormuz governance (Iran says ships would be regulated by Iran and Oman) remains unresolved and is the market-relevant sticking point.
insights.speaker: guest analyst / hosts
insights.so_what: Immediate blockade lift plus a 60-day sales license is the near-term bearish-oil catalyst; Iranian/Omani control of shipping signals Tehran intends to retain coercive leverage.
insights.quote: HE SAID THE U.S. BLOCKADE WOULD BE LIFTED IMMEDIATELY AND THERE WOULD BE POTENTIALLY A 60 DAY WINDOW IN WHICH IRAN COULD HAVE A LICENSE TO SELL WITHOUT ANY RESTRICTIONS.

insights.claim: Pakistan played the central broker role in mediating the interim US-Iran deal, while there is no evidence China was involved, though China could later be a compromise destination for processing or destroying Iran's enriched uranium.
insights.speaker: Minmin Low (Bloomberg correspondent)
insights.so_what: Pakistan's diplomatic role and a potential China uranium-custody compromise are geopolitical wildcards worth tracking for the final-deal odds.
insights.quote: PAKISTAN WAS PLAYING A MAJOR ROLE AS BROKER. BUT INTERESTING TO KEEP AN EYE ON WHAT CHINA COULD DO GOING FORWARD

themes.theme: Oil Price Path & Physical Inventory Wall

insights.claim: Despite the rally, oil is still roughly 20% higher than pre-war and Brent sits near $81-84, while WTI is closing in on $80, so markets have priced out premium but not returned to pre-war levels.
insights.speaker: David (host)
insights.so_what: Establishes the gap that still exists; a full normalization toward pre-war ~$69-70 Brent is a sizable additional downside leg if the deal holds.
insights.quote: OIL IS STILL 20% HIGHER. JUST TO KEEP THAT IN CONTEXT.

insights.claim: US strategic oil inventories are around 348 million barrels against an operational minimum near 300 million, and inventories hit a wall by end of July/early August after which the salt caverns storing them physically begin to crumble.
insights.speaker: Robert Pape, professor of political science, University of Chicago
insights.so_what: Sets a hard physical deadline (~Aug 1-7) that pressures the US to deal and is a latent bullish-oil catalyst if reopening slips; watch SPR drawdown pace.
insights.quote: THAT IS AROUND 348 MILLION BARRELS OF OIL. AT THEIR OPERATIONAL MINIMUM IS SOMEWHERE AROUND 300 MILLION BARRELS OF OIL. IT HITS A WALL AT THE END OF JULY

insights.claim: Bloomberg Intelligence forecasts that with Hormuz reopening plus a China demand slump, oil could fall back below $70.
insights.speaker: hosts (citing Bloomberg Intelligence)
insights.so_what: A sub-$70 Brent scenario reframes the trade for energy importers and oil majors; positions short oil or long EM importers benefit if BI is right.
insights.quote: WITH THE HORMUZ REOPEN, CHINA DEMAND SLUMP COULD SEND OIL BACK BELOW $70.

insights.claim: The physical oil market has adapted to the disruption through import cuts (notably China and Japan), rerouting of flows, and increased US exports, which is why prices never spiked shockingly high despite the blockade.
insights.speaker: Sara, founder & president, SVP Energy International
insights.so_what: Explains the muted price reaction and warns that adaptation buffers are finite and not a sustainable long-term solution.
insights.quote: WE HAD JAPAN AND SIGNIFICANTLY CHINA REDUCING THEIR IMPORTS. REROUTING OF OIL FLOW. AND INCREASED EXPORTS FROM THE UNITED STATES.

insights.claim: Even after a Hormuz reopening, returning to the pre-war 121-140 vessel transit level will take time, and the first oil drops will take 30-60 days to reach refineries, so the physical supply relief lags the headline.
insights.speaker: Sara, SVP Energy International / earlier guest
insights.so_what: Headline-driven oil selloffs may be premature versus physical reality; the lag keeps a floor under near-term prices and tanker/insurance dynamics in focus.
insights.quote: HOW QUICKLY CAN YOU GET THIS NORMALIZATION BACK TO 121 OR 140 VESSELS. THAT MAY TAKE SOME TIME.

insights.claim: A lasting legacy of the conflict will be permanent supply-chain adjustments, with Saudi Arabia and the UAE having used alternative logistics that bypass Hormuz, driving more investment in alternative routes and energy-security diversification.
insights.speaker: Sara, founder & president, SVP Energy International
insights.so_what: Structural tailwind for pipeline/alternative-logistics infrastructure and Gulf exporters with Hormuz-bypass capacity; reliance on the strait may stay structurally lower.
insights.quote: WE HAD SAUDI ARABIA, ABOVE ALL, AND UAE BEING ABLE TO EXPORT PART OF THAT OIL THAT WAS STRANDED INSIDE THE REGION TO THEIR ALTERNATIVE LOGISTICS THAT HAD TO BYPASS THE STRAIT OF HORMUZ.

insights.claim: Beyond oil, the Strait of Hormuz also carries LNG, petrochemicals, helium and fertilizers; Asian importers adapted to LNG disruption by burning more coal, and Japan prioritized medical-supply petrochemical production for three months.
insights.speaker: Sara, founder & president, SVP Energy International
insights.so_what: Highlights second-order commodity exposures (coal substitution, fertilizer/petrochemical supply) and food-security angles beyond the crude headline.
insights.quote: WE HAVE NOW BEEN TALKING ABOUT OIL BUT THERE ARE OTHER MOLECULES PASSING THROUGH THE STRAIT OF HORMUZ INCLUDING LNG.

insights.claim: Jet fuel prices and airfares are sticky on the way down, with flight cancellations and elevated ticket prices persisting; refiners shifted to producing more lighter products, and airlines may be slow to add capacity even as crude falls.
insights.speaker: Sara, SVP Energy International / David (host)
insights.so_what: Airline margin tailwind from falling crude lags; watch capacity decisions and the jet-fuel crack rather than assuming immediate airfare relief. Korean Air rose ~13% on the news.
insights.quote: THERE IS SOME FRICTION AND STICKINESS AROUND THAT RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THOSE TWO PRICES, AND EVEN MORE SO WHEN IT COMES TO AIRFARES

insights.claim: The Iran deal mainly lowers the probability of a Fed rate hike, giving Asian central banks room to do 25bp rather than the 50bp jumbo hikes previously discussed, even though the deal does not yet justify lower inflation expectations.
insights.speaker: Alicia Garcia Herrero, Asia Pacific economist
insights.so_what: Repricing the Fed and EM Asia hike path; the deal softens but does not remove tightening pressure, so curves stay two-sided into a heavy central-bank week.
insights.quote: WHAT THEY WILL FACTOR IN IS THE PROBABLY OVER HIKE, THE FED HIKE IS LOWER. THAT IS THE KEY.

insights.claim: Garcia Herrero argues Bank Indonesia should hike 50bp to demonstrate intent on inflation regardless of the news flow, warning that doing only 25bp risks falling behind the curve if the deal is not real, especially given Indonesia's lax fiscal and monetary stance.
insights.speaker: Alicia Garcia Herrero, Asia Pacific economist
insights.so_what: Credibility risk for Indonesia's central bank; a 25bp hike could be read as falling behind and pressure IDR and Indonesian bonds.
insights.quote: I WOULD HIKE 50 IF YOU ASKED ME BECAUSE I WOULD HAVE TO SHOW MY INTENT NO MATTER WHAT THE NEWS FLOW IS.

insights.claim: Several analysts expect 0 or at most 1 Fed hike this year, with the war's de-escalation cutting that probability toward zero, and they note Kevin Warsh joining the Fed board would not implement balance-sheet reduction quickly.
insights.speaker: Jin Qianjing, chief analyst at SWS Research
insights.so_what: Supports a lower-for-now front-end rate view; reduced QT-acceleration risk under Warsh is constructive for duration.
insights.quote: WITH THIS BREAKING NEWS, I THINK THE PRESSURE HAS GONE DOWN TO ZERO TIMES RATE HIKE.

insights.claim: SWS Research's Jin recommends buying US Treasuries at roughly 4.5% yield, judging short-term-rate pressure low, fiscal-deficit/term-premium risk controllable this year, and duration risk generally manageable at current levels.
insights.speaker: Jin Qianjing, chief analyst at SWS Research
insights.so_what: An explicit long-duration buy call near 4.5% paired with short-term low-vol bonds and AI tech equities as a barbell.
insights.quote: AT THIS CURRENT LEVEL, LIKE 4.5, I THINK THE CORPORATE RATE IS HIGH AND THE DURATION RISK IS GENERALLY CONTROLLABLE. THAT IS WHAT I THINK IS A GOOD PRICE TO BUY.

insights.claim: L&G's Ben Bennett is not a US-rates bull, expecting US inflation and thus yields to prove sticky partly because massive AI capex is creating goods inflation, and would not be surprised by a Fed hike by year-end.
insights.speaker: Ben Bennett, Asia head of investment strategy, L&G
insights.so_what: Contrasts with SWS's buy-Treasuries call; the AI-capex-as-goods-inflation channel is a non-obvious reason to stay cautious on US duration.
insights.quote: WE'VE SEEN THIS HUGE AMOUNT OF EXPENDITURE IF THE A.I. PROJECTS THAT'S BUILDING SOME GOODS INFLATION.

insights.claim: The Bank of Japan is expected to hike at this week's two-day meeting even with Governor Ueda absent for surgery, taking Japan's policy rate back to a level last seen in 1995, while dollar-yen remains above 160.
insights.speaker: David / Yvonne (hosts)
insights.so_what: A BOJ hike to 1995-era levels with USD/JPY stuck above 160 signals the yen's weakness is driven by sticky US inflation/labor dynamics, not BOJ inaction.
insights.quote: WE ARE EXPECTING THE INTEREST RATE IN JAPAN TO BE TAKEN BACK TO THE LEVEL WE LAST SAW IN 1995.

insights.claim: L&G has been incrementally adding back to Asian local bonds it had been underweight, especially Indonesia, plus risk in higher-beta Pakistan and Kenya, betting that a sustainable Iran deal and falling oil drive a relief rally with lower yields and stronger currencies.
insights.speaker: Ben Bennett, Asia head of investment strategy, L&G
insights.so_what: Concrete real-money positioning: add EM Asia/frontier local-currency bonds and FX on the energy-relief thesis ahead of confirmatory headlines.
insights.quote: WE'VE BEEN UNDERWEIGHT, A LOT OF ASIAN COUNTRY, MORALLY INDONESIA OVER THE LAST FEW MONTHS. OVER THE LAST COUPLE OF WEEKS WE HAVE BEEN ADDING

insights.claim: The Philippines and Indonesia equity/FX markets are the truest tell on whether investors believe the Iran deal, because they are oil-dependent economies that had been sold off; the Philippines rallied ~3% and Indonesia ~3% on the news.
insights.speaker: Anthony Stevens, Bloomberg markets reporter / hosts
insights.so_what: Use oil-importer EM (PHP/IDR, local equities) as the real-time conviction gauge for the deal rather than already-bid AI names in Korea/Taiwan.
insights.quote: THOSE MARKETS TELL US A LOT MORE ABOUT HOW MUCH PEOPLE BELIEVE THIS DEAL IS DONE.

insights.claim: Indonesia faces an MSCI reclassification review this month with lingering risk of a downgrade to frontier status, which originally kicked off its underperformance and could trigger further outflows if confirmed.
insights.speaker: Yvonne / David (hosts)
insights.so_what: A binary index-classification event layered on top of the oil trade; a frontier downgrade would force passive outflows independent of the Iran deal.
insights.quote: THERE IS STILL A LOT OF LINGERING CONCERNS THIS MARKET WILL BE DOWNGRADED TO FRONTIER STATUS. IF THAT IS THE CASE, THAT COULD LEAD TO EVEN MORE OUTFLOWS HERE.

insights.claim: The non-AI and traditional manufacturing sectors that were suppressed by high oil prices are the segments most likely to recover on the deal, more so than already-bid AI names.
insights.speaker: Jin Qianjing, chief analyst at SWS Research
insights.so_what: Rotation idea: oil-cost-sensitive cyclicals/manufacturers in oil-importing EM offer more incremental upside than crowded AI leaders on falling oil.
insights.quote: MAYBE THEY WILL RECOVER MORE FROM THIS NEWS BECAUSE PREVIOUSLY THEY ARE ACTUALLY SUPPRESSED BY THE FEAR OF HIGHER OIL PRICE.

insights.claim: Energy-importer economies South Korea, Japan, Taiwan and especially India stand to benefit most from a Hormuz reopening, with Korea and Japan equities already jumping on the news.
insights.speaker: Minmin Low / Alicia Garcia Herrero
insights.so_what: Identifies the cleanest oil-importer beneficiaries; India is flagged as needing it most given equity inflows running deeply negative.
insights.quote: MANY COUNTRY, PARTICULARLY THE ENERGY IMPORTERS LIKE SOUTH KOREA, JAPAN, TAIWAN, INDIA ESPECIALLY, THESE ARE COUNTRIES THAT WOULD STAND TO BENEFIT THE MOST

themes.theme: AI Tech Earnings, Valuation & IPO Wave

insights.claim: L&G tactically trimmed its tech-equity overweight about a month ago after the US tech sector's Q1 earnings rose 60%, viewing it as taking a breather, and is now looking to buy back tech on weakness including on the Iran headlines.
insights.speaker: Ben Bennett, Asia head of investment strategy, L&G
insights.so_what: Real-money signal that the tech trim is tactical not structural; dips are being bought, with conviction tied to the 60% earnings growth.
insights.quote: THAT REDUCTION WAS A TACTICAL MOVE BASED ON THE FACT THAT WE HAD AN AMAZING RUN RECENTLY IN TECHNOLOGY STOCK, PREDICATED ON Q1 EARNINGS, UP 60% ON THE TECHNOLOGY SECTOR IN THE U.S.

insights.claim: Bennett judges Korea/semiconductor earnings sustainable for only about two years, warning that chipmakers maximizing capacity now at peak profitability will bring supply online in roughly two years and call those earnings into question.
insights.speaker: Ben Bennett, Asia head of investment strategy, L&G
insights.so_what: Flags a ~2-year cyclical-peak/capacity-glut risk for memory and Korea tech despite the current run; valuations look expensive on a 5-year earnings base.
insights.quote: IF I WAS MAKING CHIP, I'D BE BILLING AS MUCH CAPACITY AS I POSSIBLY COULD RIGHT NOW WITH THE PROFITABILITY IS AMAZING. THAT'S GOING TO COME ONLINE IN TWO OUTLIERS' TIME.

insights.claim: Bennett argues China could outperform Korea over the next year, but likely because Korea plateaus rather than China accelerates, and the real catalyst for China would be specific companies making large profits, mirroring how SK Hynix/Samsung profits (not policy) drove Korea.
insights.speaker: Ben Bennett, Asia head of investment strategy, L&G
insights.so_what: Reframes the China catalyst hunt: watch for actual blowout corporate profits, not cheap valuations or policy announcements, before rotating into China tech.
insights.quote: IT MIGHT NOT BE BECAUSE CHINA ACCELERATES, BUT BECAUSE KOREA PLATEAUS.

insights.claim: The biggest risk to the tech overweight is indigestion from the wave of private companies raising money in public markets, most likely surfacing first in the credit market where AI-buildout financing could strain, leading equities to question how the buildup is funded.
insights.speaker: Ben Bennett, Asia head of investment strategy, L&G
insights.so_what: Watch AI-infrastructure credit, not equities, as the early-warning gauge; a credit indigestion event is the catalyst for a more sustainable tech correction.
insights.quote: IF WE WERE TO GET INDIGESTION AT SOME POINT, MIGHT BE VIA THE I.P.O. MARKET. MORE LIKELY AROUND THE CREDIT MARKET. THAT'S THE PLACE I COULD SEE SOME INDIGESTION.

insights.claim: US investment-grade supply is up 25% on AI-buildout issuance and has sold well, but a dispersion is emerging where heavy-issuance names trade at wide spreads while other credit is very tight, a pattern that would normally precede broad credit widening.
insights.speaker: Ben Bennett, Asia head of investment strategy, L&G
insights.so_what: Spread dispersion within IG is a leading indicator; monitor heavy AI-capex issuers' spreads for the first sign of credit stress before equities react.
insights.quote: THE SUPPLY OF THESE BONDS SUP 25%. AND THEY'VE ALL BEEN SOLD VERY WELL. BUT THERE'S A DISPERSION HAPPENING IN CREDIT MARKS.

insights.claim: The SpaceX IPO debuted smoothly without sucking liquidity out of the rest of the markets, and more large private companies raising in public markets is a key dynamic shift to watch for tech digestion risk.
insights.speaker: Yvonne / Ben Bennett
insights.so_what: Successful SpaceX float removes one near-term liquidity fear, but the pipeline of mega private listings is the swing factor for tech-market absorption.
insights.quote: THAT DEBUT SEEMED TO HAVE GONE SMOOTHLY. NOT ENOUGH TO SUCK THE LIQUIDITY OUT OF THE REST OF THE MARKETS

insights.claim: For the second half, SWS Research's Jin highlights the Chinese domestic-computing-power trend, with the DeepSeek-plus-Huawei-chips combination igniting domestic compute and creating opportunities in chips driven by domestic capex beyond US-led AI names.
insights.speaker: Jin Qianjing, chief analyst at SWS Research
insights.so_what: Domestic-capex-driven Chinese compute and chip names are pitched as a distinct AI sub-theme to own alongside US mega-caps.
insights.quote: WE CAN SEE THE COMBINATION WITH DEEPSEEK AND THE HUAWEI CHIPS, THIS IGNITED THE LAST FEW DAYS OF CHINESE DOMESTIC COMPUTING POWER.

themes.theme: China AI Apps, Models & Stock-Specific Moves

insights.claim: Ant Group is overhauling Alipay to introduce an AI agent interface that can order takeout coffee, hail a cab, and perform basic wealth-management tasks like buying into a mutual fund with user authorization, escalating its user battle with WeChat.
insights.speaker: Minmin Low (Bloomberg)
insights.so_what: Alipay's wealth-management agent capability is a differentiator versus WeChat and a new front in China's super-app AI-agent competition.
insights.quote: IT WILL ALSO BE ABLE TO DO BASIC WEALTH MANAGEMENT TASKS LIKE BUY INTO A MUTUAL FUND WITH USER AUTHORIZATION

insights.claim: Monetization is a major concern for Ant's AI push because AI agents ramp up compute usage while China's token prices are so cheap that companies will likely have to absorb much of the cost themselves.
insights.speaker: Minmin Low (Bloomberg)
insights.so_what: Cheap Chinese token pricing plus heavy compute means AI-agent features are margin-dilutive for Chinese super-apps until monetization is proven.
insights.quote: THE TOKEN PRICE IN CHINA IS SO CHEAP THESE COMPANIES WILL LIKELY HAVE TO ABSORB SOME OF THAT COST AS WELL.

insights.claim: JPMorgan downgraded MiniMax to neutral from overweight and cut its price target by more than half to 400, citing concerns over model leadership and monetization visibility after last week's repricing of its M3 model.
insights.speaker: Yvonne / David (hosts)
insights.so_what: A concrete sell-side downgrade and >50% target cut differentiates the Chinese AI-model names that the market had been lumping together.
insights.quote: THEY DOWNGRADED MINIMAX TO NEUTRAL FROM OVERWEIGHT AND SLASHING THE PRICE TARGET BY MORE THAN HALF.

insights.claim: JPMorgan kept an overweight on Zhipu and raised its price target to 1400 on stronger pricing power and earnings visibility, and Zhipu shares rallied as much as 48% on the day (settling around +29%).
insights.speaker: Yvonne / David (hosts)
insights.so_what: Investors should differentiate Chinese AI-model stocks: Zhipu's pricing power is rewarded while MiniMax's is penalized, breaking the single-basket trade.
insights.quote: THEY ARE KEEPING THE OVERWEIGHT RATING ON ZHIPU... THAT IS WHY THAT PRICE WILL RAISE TO 1400 WHEN IT COMES TO ZHIPU HERE.

themes.theme: US AI Export Controls on Anthropic

insights.claim: Anthropic suspended access to its most advanced AI models, Fable 5 and Mythos 5, following an unprecedented Trump-administration directive issued late Friday to cut off foreign nationals on national-security grounds; lacking a way to verify user citizenship, Anthropic suspended access entirely for US and foreign users alike.
insights.speaker: Avril Hong (Bloomberg, Singapore)
insights.so_what: First instance of the US controlling AI models rather than the underlying chip supply chain, a precedent that could expand AI national-security rules.
insights.quote: ANTHROPIC HAS SUSPENDED THE ACCESS FOR ITS MOST ADVANCED TOP OF THE RANGE ON ITS AI MODEL. THAT IS THE FABLE FIVE AS WELL AS MYTHOS 5 MODELS.

insights.claim: The directive reportedly followed Amazon researchers conducting jailbreak research that uncovered vulnerabilities allowing Fable 5's guardrails to be bypassed, with Amazon's team in contact with the US government before the export controls were imposed.
insights.speaker: Avril Hong (Bloomberg, citing Wall Street Journal)
insights.so_what: An Amazon-discovered jailbreak triggering government action raises competitive and security-disclosure dynamics among AI labs and their cloud partners.
insights.quote: THE DISCOVERY FOLLOWED RESEARCHERS AT AMAZON ACTUALLY UNDERTAKING JAILBREAK RESEARCH AND THAT IS HOW THE VULNERABILITIES WERE UNCOVERED.

insights.claim: The model restriction lands as Anthropic and OpenAI race toward IPOs that could come as soon as this year, raising the risk of tighter AI national-security rules and confusion for US AI policy just as developers try to prove the technology can turn a profit.
insights.speaker: Avril Hong (Bloomberg, Singapore)
insights.so_what: Regulatory model-access risk is a new overhang on AI-lab valuations and IPO timing; former AI czar David Sacks expects controls lifted once the jailbreak is fixed.
insights.quote: ANTHROPIC AT OPENAI ARE RACING TOWARDS AN IPO THAT COULD COME AS SOON AS THIS YEAR.

insights.claim: China is increasingly a dual economy: a massive export boom continues impressively while domestic demand suffers deflationary and cost-push pressure that shrinks corporate margins, with exporters relatively fine and domestic-facing firms squeezed.
insights.speaker: Alicia Garcia Herrero, Asia Pacific economist
insights.so_what: Favor China export-geared names over domestic-demand-exposed ones; the margin squeeze on domestic firms argues against broad China-consumer exposure.
insights.quote: WHOEVER IS IN THE DOMESTIC MARKET IS SUFFERING. WHOEVER IS MAINLY GEARED TOWARDS THE EXPORT MARKET IS DOING RELATIVELY WELL.

insights.claim: China has no room for real stimulus because banks have no margins and rate cuts would hurt insurers and banks, so there is no meaningful domestic stimulus coming despite the weak domestic economy.
insights.speaker: Alicia Garcia Herrero, Asia Pacific economist
insights.so_what: Tempers expectations for a China stimulus-driven domestic recovery; the bank-margin constraint limits policy easing optionality.
insights.quote: NO STIMULUS, NO REAL STIMULUS BECAUSE THERE'S NO ROOM FOR THAT. BANKS HAVE NO MARGINS.

insights.claim: Following a directive that firms cannot replace jobs with AI, China's AI labor adjustment will show up not as layoffs but as rapidly decelerating wages, fewer hours and unpaid leave in the private sector, while state-owned banks absorb workers and grow public-sector employment.
insights.speaker: Alicia Garcia Herrero, Asia Pacific economist
insights.so_what: China's AI productivity gains hit disposable income via wages/hours rather than unemployment, a distinct, demand-dampening adjustment mechanism versus the US.
insights.quote: PROBABLY PEOPLE WILL JUST HAVE FEWER HOURS. LIKE UNPAID, VOLUNTARY UNPAID LEAVE. AND THEN THE ACTUAL DISPOSABLE INCOME WILL SUFFER.

insights.claim: Taiwan's economy is clearly overheating with semiconductor-sector growth pulling talent, resources and energy from other sectors, while Korea is better positioned because it is far more diversified and less concentrated in semiconductors, giving it more room to adjust.
insights.speaker: Alicia Garcia Herrero, Asia Pacific economist
insights.so_what: Within North Asia tech, Korea offers more macro resilience than concentrated Taiwan; Taiwan's single-sector overheating and hidden wage inflation are risks.
insights.quote: TAIWAN IS CLEARLY OVERHEATING... THE OTHERS ARE SUFFERING BECAUSE... WAGES ARE INCREASING THAT SPECIFIC SECTOR AND NOT ELSEWHERE.

insights.claim: The Korean won should be stronger given Korea's current-account surplus, but outflows into the US stock market (driven by expensive US IPOs) are suppressing it, so news that an IPO is overpriced could trigger a won rebound on the back of the Iran deal.
insights.speaker: Alicia Garcia Herrero, Asia Pacific economist
insights.so_what: Trade idea: a US IPO disappointment plus the energy-relief deal could catalyze a KRW rebound that macro fundamentals already justify.
insights.quote: IN THE CASE OF KOREA, I THINK OUTFLOWS INTO U.S. STOCK MARKET IS REALLY DRIVING IT.
